figures.caption: Figure 2: Architecture of our proposed framework. An INR-driven conditioning module and a parameter-efficient adapter tailor the video diffusion model. The INR produces conditioning signals and masks, while the adapter specializes the model to the video content and motion characteristics. Both sets of weights are quantized and arithmetically encoded for compression.

Figure 7: Ablation of INR conditioning and PEFT adaptation on UVG. Enabling the INR (a) and progressively richer NOLA adapters boosts perceptual quality (b) while tracking the associated bitrate budget. However, increasing number of NOLA bases from 500 to 1000 returns only marginal improvements in perceptual quality.

Table 1: BD-metric deltas on UVG, JVET-B, and MCL-JCV. Each entry reports the BD-metric difference between the listed codec and DiV-INR. For PSNR (dB) positive values denote advantage, while for the other perceptual metrics it indicates perceptual disadvantages.

Figure 4: Rate-distortion curves on UVG, JVET-B, and MCL-JCV. Our approach (DiV-INR) is compared with traditional codecs (H.265/HEVC [31], H.266/VVC [3]), INR or neural video codecs (HiNeRV [19], DCVC-FM [22]), and diffusion-based generative codecs (Relic et al. [28]) using PSNR, MS-SSIM, LPIPS, DISTS, and FID across matched bitrates, highlighting the perceptual gains delivered by our INR-conditioned video diffusion approach.

Figure 5: Qualitative comparison on UVG, JVET-B, and MCL-JCV. Representative crops show that our approach preserves high-frequency structure that Relic et al. [28], HiNeRV [19], DCVC-FM [22], and VVC [3] lose at similar bitrates. Best viewed digitally.
